pr.title: ft: Match ftdevice and partially match ftCo_DownBound
pr.author: itsgrimetime

summary: Matched ftCo_800C0764 in ftdevice.c to 100% and made a small partial-match improvement to ftCo_800978D4 in ftCo_DownBound.c, reported as 98.8%. The ftdevice work replaced a NOT_IMPLEMENTED stub with a concrete registration routine for ground/device collision callbacks, updated its header prototype, and recorded the match in scratches.txt. The DownBound tweak used explicit Fighter offset accesses and a direct efAsync_Spawn call instead of the prior inlineA0 wrapper and named coll_data fields to improve codegen.
what_changed: Added scratches.txt entry marking ftCo_800C0764 as 100% MATCHED by itsgrimetime.
Changed ftdevice literal extern declarations from char* to char[] for ftDevice_803C6B18, ftDevice_803C6B40, ftDevice_803C6B4C, ftDevice_803C6B78, and ftCo_804D3C18.
Implemented ftCo_800C0764 as void ftCo_800C0764(Ground_GObj* arg0, u32 arg1, void* arg2), replacing an UNK_RET/UNK_PARAMS NOT_IMPLEMENTED stub.
ftCo_800C0764 now scans ft_80459A68 slots 1 and 2 for an empty ground field, stores the Ground_GObj pointer, type, and active_cb, increments ftDevice_BuryThingCount, and returns.
ftCo_800C0764 now reports and asserts on overflow with OSReport("fighter chk device coll func num over!\n") and __assert("ftdevice.c", 0x6FU, "0").
Updated src/melee/ft/ftdevice.h prototype for ftCo_800C0764 to use concrete argument types.

Modified ftCo_800978D4 in ftCo_DownBound.c by replacing an 8-byte initialized dummy array with u8 pad[16], reading floor normal components through explicit offsets 0x844 and 0x848 from Fighter, loading a JObj pointer from offset 0x5e8, and calling efAsync_Spawn directly with effect id 0x406.
The prior ftCo_800978D4 form used fp->coll_data.floor.normal fields and inlineA0(gobj, 4, 1030, FtPart_TopN, &param); the new form appears tuned for closer assembly matching.
smart_moves: Concretized ftCo_800C0764's signature in both source and header once the real parameter roles were evident: Ground_GObj, type/id as u32, and callback pointer.

Preserved matching-sensitive pointer traversal in ftCo_800C0764 by using both a base pointer and an incremented ptr while writing via base[i + 1], matching likely original codegen more closely than a simplified array loop might.
Used char[] rather than char* for literal extern declarations, which is often important for matching string/literal references because it changes how the compiler treats the symbol.
For ftCo_800978D4, preferred explicit byte-offset loads and a direct efAsync_Spawn call when named struct fields and an inline wrapper did not produce the desired code shape.
Kept the existing behavior tail-call/sequence by still calling ftCo_800976A4(gobj) after spawning the effect.
decomp_lessons: When matching old CodeWarrior output, a semantically cleaner loop over an array may not match; preserving pointer temporaries and write expressions such as base[i + 1] can be necessary.
Literal declarations should use array types, not pointer types, when the underlying symbol is an embedded string/literal object rather than a pointer variable.

A function can be considered matched even when nearby structures are not fully typed; concrete function signatures and carefully chosen local variable shapes are enough to remove NOT_IMPLEMENTED stubs.
Partial matches may require falling back from known struct field names to explicit Fighter byte offsets when the field access path affects register allocation or instruction selection.

Inlining or wrapper helpers can hurt matching if the original code directly called the underlying function; replacing inlineA0 with efAsync_Spawn improved the ftCo_800978D4 match percentage according to the PR body.
naming_conventions: Unmatched or partially understood fighter common functions retained address-style names such as ftCo_800C0764 and ftCo_800978D4.
Global data retained address-style names such as ft_80459A68 and ft_804D6570.
Literal symbols used address-style names with subsystem prefixes, e.g. ftDevice_803C6B18 and ftCo_804D3C18.
Unknown structs retained placeholder names such as struct ftDeviceUnk3.
The callback field name active_cb was used for the third ftDevice registration argument, indicating a likely device active callback role without overnaming it.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Changed dummy local stack padding from initialized u8 _[8] = { 0 } to uninitialized u8 pad[16] in ftCo_800978D4.
Used explicit pointer arithmetic casts like *(f32*)((u8*)fp + 0x844), *(f32*)((u8*)fp + 0x848), and *(HSD_JObj***)((u8*)fp + 0x5e8) to shape loads.
Replaced a wrapper/inline helper call with direct efAsync_Spawn(gobj, (u8*)gobj->user_data + 0x60c, 4, 0x406, *jobj_ptr, &param).
Declared string literal externs as char arrays to match relocation/literal access behavior.
Used a two-iteration loop over ft_80459A68 slots beginning at base + 1, with explicit overflow OSReport and __assert paths, for ftCo_800C0764.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR body explicitly states the matching outcomes: ftCo_800C0764 at 100% and ftCo_800978D4 at 98.8%.
searchable_terms: fighter chk device coll func num over
follow_up_queries: Look for later PRs that rename ftCo_800C0764 or struct ftDeviceUnk3 fields after the callback semantics became clearer.
Check whether ftCo_800978D4 was later fully matched without explicit Fighter byte offsets.
Search for other ftdevice functions such as ftCo_800C07F8 to compare callback registration and overflow handling patterns.
Investigate whether offsets 0x844, 0x848, 0x5e8, and 0x60c are now represented by named Fighter fields in current headers.
